contentParagraphs.en: Hannah and Max have always split their money equally even when one has earned considerably more than the other. "We've always wanted to do things 50/50," Max, 31, says. "We made a commitment to each other that what's mine is yours, and vice versa."The couple got married after two years together, when Max was earning £70,000 a year working in tech, and Hannah, who works in the charity sector, was earning considerably less.Their salaries go into a joint account and their mortgage, bills, food, petrol and other household costs are paid out of this.Each month they take the same amount for personal spending.If one of them wants to buy clothes, make-up or go to the pub they can do that without feeling watched and "it means we aren't accountable to each other for those things", says Max.They also divide money into different pots, including savings and smaller funds for predictable spending like haircuts.The couple's system of sharing money remained the same even when Max was made redundant last year and they had to rely on Hannah's salary of between £40,000 and £60,000. "There was shock and panic initially," Hannah, who did not want to disclose her precise salary, says. "But we sat down and really looked through where we could cut things out."They cancelled gym memberships and Sky TV and went through their finances to make sure they could cover their mortgage and bills on a lower income.Max had trained at culinary school instead of doing A-levels and university, but had moved away from hospitality because he felt it would not pay enough.When he was made redundant he wanted to use the opportunity to create his own business in something he genuinely enjoyed - but Hannah had reservations."At first I told him to set this up alongside getting another job but we quickly realised the business would take a lot of time and energy," she says."We ran the numbers and decided that this was the right moment to take the risk given we don't have kids and no one else is depending on us."Max spent £20,000 from his redundancy package buying and converting a horsebox trailer into a pizza truck, which now caters for weddings, parties and pop-ups.He says the money he invested was their agreed limit to make the business work and if it failed he would find another job rather than putting in more.The business now brings in between £4,000 and £6,000 a month in revenue and Max pays himself a salary that covers the mortgage.
contentParagraphs.zh: 汉娜和麦克斯总是平分他们的钱，即使其中一方的收入比另一方多得多。 “我们一直希望以 50/50 的比例做事，”31 岁的马克斯说道。 “我们向彼此做出承诺，我的就是你的，反之亦然。”这对夫妇在一起两年后结婚，当时马克斯在科技行业工作，年薪为 7 万英镑，而在慈善部门工作的汉娜的收入要少得多。他们的工资存入一个联名账户，抵押贷款、账单、食品、汽油和其他家庭费用都从中支付。每个月他们拿同样的金额用于个人支出。如果其中一人想买东西穿衣服、化妆或去酒吧，他们可以在不感到被监视的情况下做到这一点，“这意味着我们不必为这些事情对彼此负责”，麦克斯说。他们还将钱分成不同的罐子，包括储蓄和用于理发等可预测支出的小额资金。即使去年麦克斯被解雇，夫妇俩的分钱制度仍然保持不变，他们不得不依靠汉娜 40,000 至 60,000 英镑的工资。 “一开始大家感到震惊和恐慌，”不愿透露具体工资的汉娜说。 “但我们坐下来认真研究了哪些地方可以删减。”他们取消了健身房会员资格和天空电视台，并检查了他们的财务状况，以确保他们能够以较低的收入支付抵押贷款和账单。麦克斯在烹饪学校接受过培训，而不是参加高级水平考试和大学，但他离开了酒店业，因为他觉得这薪水不够。当他被解雇时，他想利用这个机会开创自己的事业，从事他真正喜欢的事情——但汉娜有所保留。“起初我告诉他。 “我们在找另一份工作的同时设立这个项目，但我们很快意识到这项业务需要花费大量的时间和精力，”她说。“我们计算了数据，认为现在是冒险的好时机，因为我们没有孩子，而且没有其他人依赖我们。”马克斯从他的裁员计划中花费了 2 万英镑购买了一辆马箱拖车，并将其改装成一辆披萨卡车，现在可以满足婚礼、派对和快闪店的需求。他说，他投资的资金是他们商定的让业务运转的限额，如果如果失败了，他会另谋出路，而不是投入更多。现在，这家公司每月的收入在 4,000 到 6,000 英镑之间，马克斯给自己支付的工资足以支付抵押贷款。

articles.title: Trump cuts target federal advisory boards. What does it mean for agencies like NASA?
articles.summary: The Trump administration has terminated or suspended more than 100 federal advisory committees. Former members say agencies like NASA now have less accountability to stakeholders and the public.
articles.titleZh: 特朗普削减了联邦顾问委员会的目标。这对于 NASA 等机构意味着什么？
articles.summaryZh: 特朗普政府已终止或暂停了 100 多个联邦咨询委员会。前成员表示，像美国宇航局这样的机构现在对利益相关者和公众的责任减少了。

contentParagraphs.en: The Trump administration has terminated or suspended more than 100 federal advisory committees. Former members say agencies like NASA now have less accountability to stakeholders and the public.
contentParagraphs.zh: 特朗普政府已终止或暂停了 100 多个联邦咨询委员会。前成员表示，像美国宇航局这样的机构现在对利益相关者和公众的责任减少了。

contentParagraphs.en: JUANA SUMMERS, HOST: The Trump administration is working to fundamentally change how scientific research happens in the U.S. One of its strategies is eliminating or freezing dozens of panels of independent advisers to the government. NPR's Katia Riddle brings us this story about the impact this has had on federal agencies like NASA.KATIA RIDDLE, BYLINE: Federal advisory committees are a hallmark of American government. They bring together experts from a wide range of fields to help think through tough questions. How do we develop better cancer treatments? What should children be vaccinated against? How do we detect asteroids before they hit the Earth? One such expert is Kelly Holley-Bockelmann. Until recently, she was a professor of astrophysics at Vanderbilt University. She says volunteering for these federal advisory committees came with a lot of benefits.KELLY HOLLEY-BOCKELMANN: I really liked to learn about how NASA worked and how it chose its missions and what all was part of a mission portfolio and how a mission came to fruition.RIDDLE: Holley-Bockelmann was on the Astrophysics Advisory Committee. That was one of several NASA committees that were terminated soon after the start of the second Trump term. Before that, she spent years advising on issues relevant to astrophysics.HOLLEY-BOCKELMANN: Then when I finally understood how things worked, I felt like I could help to bring the community's concerns to light to NASA.RIDDLE: Since the committee ended, she's been worried about key NASA projects. For example, the LISA mission.HOLLEY-BOCKELMANN: It's this amazing gravitational wave telescope. It's actually as big as the sun, and it's designed to basically detect the gravitational waves from black holes merging.RIDDLE: Holley-Bockelmann says she and other committee members were concerned that NASA was not fulfilling its commitments to international partners on this mission. Had they been able to continue their work, they would have stressed that to the agency.HOLLEY-BOCKELMANN: There were a couple times where we offered expertise that was not listened to (laughter). But nonetheless, we felt it was really important to get it down on record.RUSS PAULSEN: What advisory committees can do is give a diversity of viewpoints and an independence of viewpoints that no one organization will ever have.RIDDLE: Russ Paulsen is the CEO of Research America. That's a group that does advocacy around science policy. Paulsen says that eliminating these groups leaves a crucial deficit.PAULSEN: We need to have choices and we need to have a diversity of opinions to get the best science done.RIDDLE: The White House said in a statement that committees that were ended were, quote, "redundant, unnecessary or ineffective." NASA called these eliminations a restructuring, saying they had merged several committees into one and writing that NASA, quote, "has remained committed to continuing to engage with the science community in a variety of ways," unquote. Juan Meza has been on a number of committees advising on issues of engineering and science.JUAN MEZA: I mean, everybody who I've ever served with on these committees, they take their role very seriously.RIDDLE: Meza is a professor of applied mathematics at University of California, Merced. He says, often, that being on a committee means making recommendations to federal agencies that aren't popular or politically favorable. He recalls one such decision he was part of years ago advising against a significant investment in supercomputers that leadership at the Department of Energy wanted to make.MEZA: It was not a well-received report, but again, the advisory committees are there to give independent expert advice.RIDDLE: Was it difficult to make that decision? Were - did you feel like maybe we should tell them what they want to hear?MEZA: There was a lot of pressure. I'm being very careful since you're recording this.(LAUGHTER)MEZA: It was a challenging time, and it was not an easy decision to make.RIDDLE: Still, he says, the committee advised the DOE not to make the investment. Reluctantly, he says, leadership followed their advice. Meza stresses that had their jobs been on the line, they might have made a different decision.MEZA: The fact that we were independent, I think, gave us a certain amount of leeway to say, no, no, no, no, no, this is - we've got to go with what we think is right.RIDDLE: In the absence of these committees, Meza fears, federal leaders will hear less from independent experts and more from people who tell them exactly what they want to hear. Katia Riddle, NPR News.(SOUNDBITE OF LOLA YOUNG SONG, "REVOLVE AROUND YOU")
contentParagraphs.zh: 主持人胡安娜·萨默斯：特朗普政府正在努力从根本上改变美国科学研究的方式。其战略之一是取消或冻结数十个政府独立顾问小组。 NPR 的卡蒂亚·里德尔为我们带来了这个故事，讲述了这对美国宇航局等联邦机构的影响。卡蒂亚·里德尔连线：联邦咨询委员会是美国政府的标志。他们汇集了来自各个领域的专家，帮助思考棘手的问题。我们如何开发更好的癌症治疗方法？儿童应该接种什么疫苗？我们如何在小行星撞击地球之前探测到它们？凯利·霍利-博克尔曼（Kelly Holley-Bockelmann）就是这样的一位专家。直到最近，她还是范德比尔特大学的天体物理学教授。她说，为这些联邦咨询委员会做志愿者有很多好处。 凯利·霍利-博克曼：我真的很喜欢了解 NASA 是如何工作的，它是如何选择任务的，以及任务组合的一部分是什么，以及任务是如何实现的。谜语：霍利-博克曼是天体物理学咨询委员会的成员。这是特朗普第二个任期开始后不久就被终止的几个美国宇航局委员会之一。在此之前，她花了数年时间就与天体物理学相关的问题提供建议。 霍利-博克曼：然后，当我最终了解事情是如何运作时，我觉得我可以帮助将社区的担忧暴露给 NASA。 谜语：自从委员会结束以来，她一直担心 NASA 的关键项目。例如，LISA 任务。 HOLLEY-BOCKELMANN：这是一个令人惊叹的引力波望远镜。它实际上和太阳一样大，它的设计目的主要是探测黑洞合并产生的引力波。 谜语：霍利-博克曼说，她和其他委员会成员担心美国宇航局没有履行其对国际合作伙伴在这次任务中的承诺。如果他们能够继续工作，他们就会向该机构强调这一点。 霍利-博克尔曼：有几次我们提供了专业知识，但没有被听取（笑声）。但尽管如此，我们认为将其记录下来确实很重要。 拉斯·保尔森：咨询委员会可以做的是提供多样性的观点和观点的独立性，这是任何一个组织都不会拥有的。谜语：拉斯·保尔森是美国研究公司的首席执行官。这是一个围绕科学政策进行宣传的团体。保尔森说，消除这些群体会留下严重的赤字。 保尔森：我们需要有选择，我们需要有多元化的意见才能完成最好的科学研究。 谜语：白宫在一份声明中表示，被终止的委员会是“多余的、不必要的或无效的”。美国宇航局称这些取消是一种重组，称他们已将几个委员会合并为一个委员会，并写道，美国宇航局“仍然致力于以各种方式继续与科学界互动”（未引述）。胡安·梅萨 (Juan Meza) 曾在多个委员会就工程和科学问题提供咨询。 胡安·梅萨 (JUAN MEZA)：我的意思是，我曾在这些委员会中共事过的每个人，他们都非常认真地对待自己的角色。 里德尔：梅萨 (Juan Meza) 是加州大学默塞德分校的应用数学教授。他经常说，成为委员会成员意味着向联邦机构提出不受欢迎或政治上不有利的建议。他回忆起几年前他参与的一项此类决定，建议反对能源部领导层想要做出的对超级计算机的重大投资。 MEZA：这不是一份受欢迎的报告，但同样，咨询委员会提供独立的专家建议。RIDDLE：做出这个决定很难吗？你觉得也许我们应该告诉他们他们想听什么？ MEZA：压力很大。自从你录制这个以来我就非常小心了。（笑声）MEZA：那是一个充满挑战的时刻，这不是一个容易做出的决定。RIDDLE：不过，他说，委员会建议能源部不要进行投资。他说，领导层不情愿地听从了他们的建议。梅萨强调，如果他们的工作受到威胁，他们可能会做出不同的决定。 梅萨：我认为，我们是独立的，这一事实给了我们一定的余地，可以说，不，不，不，不，不，这是 - 我们必须遵循我们认为正确的事情。 里德尔：梅萨担心，如果没有这些委员会，联邦领导人将减少从独立专家那里听到的声音，而更多地从那些准确地告诉他们他们想听到的人那里听到。卡蒂亚·里德尔 (Katia Riddle)，NPR 新闻。（LOLA YOUNG SONG 的原声片段，“围绕着你旋转”）

articles.title: US and Iran trade more strikes in Gulf as fears of escalation mount
articles.summary: It comes as Iran condemned the US plan to use frozen Iranian assets to pay for war damages.
articles.titleZh: 随着对局势升级的担忧加剧，美国和伊朗在海湾地区进行了更多打击
articles.summaryZh: 与此同时，伊朗谴责美国计划使用冻结的伊朗资产来支付战争损失。

contentParagraphs.en: Iran says it has carried out drone strikes on US military facilities and bases across the Middle East in retaliation for the 13th consecutive night of American attacks on the country.Explosions were reported along Iran's Gulf coast, and in western, northern and central areas. Four people were killed and five injured in an attack in the south-west, Iranian state media said.Iran later announced strikes on US-allied Kuwait, while sirens sounded in Bahrain and Jordan, and flights were suspended at a major Iraqi airport after a reported drone crash.It comes as Iran condemned the US plan to use frozen Iranian assets to pay for war damages, in a sign of further escalation between the two countries.On Thursday, US President Donald Trump said damages to ships and cargo in the Strait of Hormuz would be paid for by "Iranian Money that the United States has in its possession, and controls"."These damages may be very substantial but, nevertheless, this is the fair and equitable thing to do," he posted on his Truth Social platform.Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi responded by saying that seizing another country's assets to "pay for unrelated future claims is an incendiary precedent"."Ensuing chaos will not be pretty or peaceful," he added.In June, the US and Iran agreed on a 14-point Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) to halt military operations and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, as well as to reach an agreement to end the war over the following 60 days.But three weeks after it was signed, Trump declared the ceasefire "over" after Iranian attacks on ships in the strait, and US strikes in response.Point 11 of the MoU detailed that the "United States of America undertakes to make fully available for use the frozen or restricted funds and assets of the Islamic Republic of Iran".As the US pushes to regain control over the Strait of Hormuz, there's growing concern over new threats to shipping in the Red Sea from Yemen's Iran-backed Houthis.That has helped push the price of crude oil back over $100 (£74.96) a barrel - the highest level since May.Trump separately on Thursday vowed "major military punishment" against Iran for Houthi attacks.He later told the Axios news website he was considering restarting major combat operations in Iran and launching strikes "bigger than ever before". Tensions have been escalating between the US and Iran since the start of July.On Thursday night, the US military says it carried out strikes to degrade Iran's ability to threaten civilian shipping in regional waters. US Central Command (Centcom) said it had targeted "Iranian military command centers, drone storage facilities, communication networks, coastal surveillance sites and maritime capabilities".Iran's army claimed it had launched attacks on US bases in Kuwait, while Bahrain's Interior Ministry reported that its air sirens had been sounded and Iranian state media also said alarms had been activated in Jordan.The AFP and Reuters news agencies both reported a drone crash at Iraq's Erbil International Airport, causing a temporary stoppage. Flights have since resumed.
contentParagraphs.zh: 伊朗表示，它对美国在中东各地的军事设施和基地进行了无人机袭击，以报复美国连续13晚对该国发动袭击。据报道，伊朗墨西哥湾沿岸以及西部、北部和中部地区都发生了爆炸。伊朗官方媒体称，西南部发生的袭击造成四人死亡、五人受伤。伊朗随后宣布对美国盟友科威特发动袭击，巴林和约旦拉响了警报，伊拉克一个主要机场的航班在据报道发生无人机坠毁事件后暂停。伊朗谴责美国计划使用冻结的伊朗资产来支付战争损失，这表明两国之间的局势进一步升级。周四，美国总统唐纳德·特朗普表示，伊朗海峡的船只和货物受损。霍尔木兹将由“美国拥有和控制的伊朗资金”来支付。他在自己的“真相社交”平台上发帖称，“这些损失可能非常巨大，但无论如何，这是公平公正的事情。”伊朗外交部长阿巴斯·阿拉格奇回应称，扣押另一个国家的资产来“支付不相关的未来索赔是一个煽动性的先例”。“随之而来的混乱不会是美丽或和平的，”他补充道。美国和伊朗就一项 14 点谅解备忘录 (MOU) 达成一致，停止军事行动并重新开放霍尔木兹海峡，并就在接下来的 60 天内结束战争达成协议。但签署三周后，特朗普在伊朗袭击海峡内的船只以及美国进行反击后宣布停火“结束”。谅解备忘录的第 11 点详细说明，“美利坚合众国承诺充分使用冻结或限制的停火协议”。伊朗伊斯兰共和国的资金和资产“。随着美国努力重新控制霍尔木兹海峡，人们越来越担心也门伊朗支持的胡塞武装对红海航运造成的新威胁。这帮助原油价格回升至每桶100美元（74.96英镑）以上，为5月以来的最高水平。周四，特朗普单独誓言对伊朗针对胡塞武装的袭击进行“重大军事惩罚”。他后来告诉Axios新闻网站，他考虑重启在伊朗的重大作战行动并发动“比以往任何时候都更大规模”的打击。自七月初以来，美国和伊朗之间的紧张局势不断升级。周四晚间，美国军方表示，发动袭击是为了削弱伊朗威胁地区水域民用航运的能力。美国中央司令部（Centcom）表示，其目标是“伊朗军事指挥中心、无人机储存设施、通信网络、沿海监视站点和海上能力”。伊朗军队声称对美国在科威特的基地发动了袭击，巴林内政部报道称，其空中警报已拉响，伊朗官方媒体也称约旦已拉响警报。法新社和路透社均报道称，伊拉克埃尔比勒国际机场发生无人机坠毁事件，导致机场暂时停运。此后航班已恢复。

articles.title: Elon Musk says he got ‘carried away’ with Trump – but still holds on to contentious political views
articles.summary:
In interview, billionaire says instead of running ‘Doge’ he should’ve ‘worked on my companies’ and shares extreme predictions
Elon Musk has admitted he became too consumed by politics during his volatile alliance with Donald Trump and his brief appointment as the president’s fina
articles.titleZh: 埃隆·马斯克表示，他对特朗普“着迷”，但仍坚持有争议的政治观点
articles.summaryZh:
在采访中，亿万富翁表示，他不应该经营“Doge”，而应该“为我的公司工作”，并分享了极端的预测
埃隆·马斯克承认，在与唐纳德·特朗普的不稳定联盟以及短暂任命为总统首席财务官期间，他变得过于沉迷于政治。

contentParagraphs.en: Elon Musk has admitted he became too consumed by politics during his volatile alliance with Donald Trump and his brief appointment as the president’s financial axman, running the so-called “department of government efficiency” (Doge).The world’s richest person, and briefly its first trillionaire after his SpaceX company floated on the New York stock exchange in June, made the confession in a wide-ranging interview with the Economist published on Thursday. He shared his positions on subjects from foreign wars to AI, and made a number of eye-opening predictions for the future.“I got carried away, frankly,” Musk said of his relationship with the president, whom he helped get elected in 2024 with $200m in financial backing, then served during his controversial tenure as head of Doge, overseeing $150bn in “harmful” budget cuts and forcing tens of thousands of people out of federal jobs.The owner of Tesla did not expand on the statement, but appeared cognizant of the impact of his dalliance with the levers of power in Washington DC.In December, he offered in a podcast with Katie Miller, wife of Trump’s White House adviser Stephen Miller, that his wrecking-ball approach to the federal government had been only partially successful, and that given the opportunity he probably wouldn’t do it again.“I think instead of doing Doge, I would have basically worked on my companies,” he said at the time.Talking to the Economist, however, he appeared to relish the attention his political involvement had garnered, and the influence he believed he exerted. “People don’t realize that what I’m saying will come to pass,” he said, the outlet referring to Musk – at least in his own mind – as a modern-day Cassandra.The interview, a 90-minute conversation for the publication’s video platform The Insider, recorded at a Tesla factory in Texas, was Musk’s first since the SpaceX flotation, in which his personal wealth briefly topped $1.25tn before dropping back to a relatively penurious $750bn.He reflected on moving from “exhilaration to terror” in a single day, but was not referring to the SpaceX share price. Instead, he was talking about artificial intelligence, which he believes will ultimately render work by humans “optional”.AI systems and robots will eventually handle most digital and physical jobs, he said, and the merger of his own AI venture with SpaceX will allow him to develop systems to perform cognitive tasks such as software programming while Tesla manufactures humanoids.Musk, the Economist said, plans to power his AI venture with datacenters in space, which experts have warned will be “catastrophic” for Earth.Within five years, Musk said, AI intelligence systems may surpass the sum of all human intelligence. Within 10 years, robots in the workplace will help usher in an era of such “amazing abundance” that money will become meaningless.It was, however, Musk’s political views, and predictions, that were perhaps the most contentious parts of the interview. The UK, a country he has not visited for years, but whose politics, especially on immigration and race, have become a recent obsession, will become engulfed in a civil war within 20 years, he claimed.Asked if he was anti-Muslim, Musk attacked the questioner, a policy of diversion similar to that which has served Trump well, and which he has employed previously, notably calling the Guardian “irrelevant” when it decided to withdraw from his “toxic” social media platform X.“I’m against rape and murder. I’m against the imposition of rules and laws that are contrary to what we’ve come to accept in the west,” he said. “It’s a crying shame that traditional media, like you, don’t recognize this.”He described the media’s characterization of Europe’s far right as “false, misleading and nonsense”, and argued that it was made up of “normal people” seeking safe cities, secure borders and sensible spending, the Economist said.In an analysis piece accompanying the interview, the outlet acknowledged that Musk was a divisive figure. “His political views, disseminated to his 240m followers on X, strike many as plain-speaking and strike many more, including The Economist, as plainly bigoted,” it wrote.“But he is also one of a handful of men who are pioneering AI and who thereby have an outsize influence on its trajectory. When he speaks, he reflects the debates they are having. His datacentres in space could power AI’s future.“For all his political polemics, he has a record of being right about technology in fields such as electric cars, rockets and satellite communications that confounded other engineers and entrepreneurs.”Another Musk prediction: after AI destroys jobs, there may need to be a large-scale wealth redistribution to create “universal high income” for humans.He said he would be willing to pay “trillions in tax” then, conversely, argued that money will become irrelevant inside 10 years. In which eventuality, he said, governments should “issue people cheques”, because, he said, amid an infinite supply of goods and services produced by machines, deflation will be a bigger problem than inflation.He also shared thoughts about the Ukraine war, in which his Starlink satellites have been used to help that country’s military against Russia’s aggression. Despite this, Musk said any peace deal should include territorial gains for Russia.The interview concluded with an assessment by Musk of his achievements and ambitious current projects, in AI and in space, a field in which he has also made a number of bold predictions. He said in 2024 that humans could set foot on Mars by 2028 (currently Nasa is targeting 2028 for a return to the moon, which many analysts think is unlikely).The universe, Musk said, according to the Economist, could be a computer simulation created by aliens. “The things I’m doing are so preposterous that it’s hard to believe they’re real,” he said.
contentParagraphs.zh: 埃隆·马斯克 (Elon Musk) 承认，在与唐纳德·特朗普 (Donald Trump) 不稳定的联盟期间，以及短暂被任命为总统的财务斧头，负责管理所谓的“政府效率部” (Doge) 期间，他变得过于沉迷于政治。 这位世界首富，以及 6 月份 SpaceX 公司在纽约证券交易所上市后短暂成为的第一位亿万富翁，在周四发表的《经济学人》采访中承认了这一点。他分享了他在从外国战争到人工智能等话题上的立场，并对未来做出了一些令人大开眼界的预测。“坦率地说，我得意忘形了，”马斯克在谈到他与总统的关系时说道。他在 2024 年以 2 亿美元的财政支持帮助总统当选，然后在他担任总督期间备受争议的任期内，监督了 1500 亿美元的“有害”预算削减，迫使数万人失业。特斯拉的所有者没有详细说明声明中，但似乎意识到他与华盛顿特区权力杠杆的调情所造成的影响。去年 12 月，他在与特朗普白宫顾问斯蒂芬·米勒的妻子凯蒂·米勒的播客中表示，他对联邦政府的破坏性手段只取得了部分成功，而且如果有机会，他可能不会再这样做了。“我认为，我不会做 Doge，基本上会为我的公司工作，”他当时说道。然而，作为一名经济学家，他似乎很享受他的政治参与所获得的关注，以及他认为自己所施加的影响力。他说：“人们没有意识到我所说的将会实现。”该媒体将马斯克称为现代的卡桑德拉（至少在他自己看来）。这次采访是该出版物视频平台 The Insider 的 90 分钟对话，在德克萨斯州的一家特斯拉工厂录制，是马斯克自 SpaceX 上市以来的首次采访，在 SpaceX 上市后，他的个人财富曾一度突破 1.25 万亿美元，然后又回落至相对匮乏的 7500 亿美元。一天之内从“兴奋变为恐惧”，但指的并不是 SpaceX 的股价。相反，他谈论的是人工智能，他认为人工智能最终将使人类的工作变得“可选”。他说，人工智能系统和机器人最终将处理大多数数字和物理工作，他自己的人工智能企业与 SpaceX 的合并将使他能够开发执行认知任务的系统，例如软件编程，而特斯拉则制造人形机器人。《经济学人》称，马斯克计划通过太空数据中心为他的人工智能企业提供动力，专家警告这对地球来说将是“灾难性的”。马斯克说，人工智能在五年内智能系统可能超过所有人类智能的总和。 10年内，工作场所中的机器人将帮助迎来一个“惊人的丰富”时代，金钱将变得毫无意义。然而，马斯克的政治观点和预测可能是采访中最具争议的部分。他声称，英国已经多年没有访问过，但该国的政治，特别是在移民和种族问题上，最近已成为人们关注的焦点，该国将在 20 年内陷入内战。 当被问及他是否反穆斯林时，马斯克攻击了发问者，这种转移政策类似于特朗普曾经使用过的政策，尤其是当《卫报》决定退出他的“有毒”社交媒体平台 X 时，他称《卫报》“无关紧要”。反对强奸和谋杀。我反对强加与我们西方所接受的规则和法律相反的规则和法律。” “像你们这样的传统媒体没有认识到这一点，真是太遗憾了。”《经济学人》称，他将媒体对欧洲极右翼的描述描述为“虚假、误导性和无稽之谈”，并认为这是由寻求安全城市、安全边界和合理支出的“普通人”组成的。在采访附带的一篇分析文章中，该媒体承认马斯克是一个分裂人物。文章写道：“他的政治观点在 X 上传播给他的 2.4 亿粉丝，让很多人觉得很直白，也让包括《经济学人》在内的更多人觉得明显偏执。但他也是人工智能先驱者之一，因此对人工智能的发展轨迹产生了巨大影响。当他讲话时，他反映了他们正在进行的辩论。他在太空中的数据中心可以为人工智能的未来提供动力。” “在电动汽车、火箭和卫星通信等领域的技术上，他的记录是正确的，这让其他工程师和企业家感到困惑。”马斯克的另一个预测是：人工智能摧毁就业机会后，可能需要进行大规模的财富再分配，为人类创造“普遍的高收入”。他表示，他愿意支付“数万亿美元的税收”，但相反，他认为金钱将在 10 年内变得无关紧要。他说，在这种情况下，政府应该“向人们发放”他说，因为在机器生产的商品和服务无限供应的情况下，通货紧缩将是比通货膨胀更大的问题。他还分享了对乌克兰战争的看法，在这场战争中，他的星链卫星被用来帮助该国军队抵御俄罗斯的侵略。尽管如此，马斯克表示，任何和平协议都应该包括俄罗斯的领土收益。采访的最后，马斯克评估了他在人工智能和太空领域的成就和雄心勃勃的当前项目，在这个领域，他也取得了许多成就。他在 2024 年大胆预测，人类可能会在 2028 年踏上火星（目前美国宇航局的目标是 2028 年重返月球，许多分析师认为这不太可能）。据《经济学人》报道，马斯克表示，宇宙可能是外星人创造的计算机模拟，“我所做的事情是如此荒谬，以至于很难相信它们是真实的。”

articles.title: Trump to impose double-digit tariffs on dozens of countries
articles.summary: The United States will slap taxes of 10% to 12.5% on imports from 60 countries accounting for 99% of U.S. imports, charging that they have inadequately enforced bans on goods produced by forced labor.
articles.titleZh: 特朗普将对数十个国家征收两位数关税
articles.summaryZh: 美国将对来自60个国家（占美国进口总量的99%）的进口产品征收10%至12.5%的关税，指责它们对强迫劳动生产的商品执行禁令不力。

contentParagraphs.en: President Donald Trump speaks during an event to announce new tariffs in the Rose Garden at the White House on April 2, 2025, in Washington.
contentParagraphs.zh: 唐纳德·特朗普总统于 2025 年 4 月 2 日在华盛顿白宫玫瑰园举行的宣布新关税的活动中发表讲话。

contentParagraphs.en: WASHINGTON — President Donald Trump is going ahead with new double-digit tariffs on dozens of U.S. trading partners just as the clock runs out Friday on stopgap levies he imposed after a stinging defeat at the Supreme Court.
contentParagraphs.zh: 华盛顿——唐纳德·特朗普总统在最高法院惨败后征收的权宜之计已于周五到期，他将继续对数十个美国贸易伙伴征收新的两位数关税。

contentParagraphs.en: The United States will slap taxes of 10% to 12.5% on imports from 60 countries accounting for 99% of U.S. imports, charging that they have inadequately enforced bans on goods produced by forced labor.
contentParagraphs.zh: 美国将对来自60个国家（占美国进口总量的99%）的进口产品征收10%至12.5%的关税，指责它们对强迫劳动生产的商品执行禁令不力。

contentParagraphs.en: "The United States has had a forced labor import ban for nearly a century, and rigorously enforces it; it's well past time for our trading partners to do the same," said U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer. "Today's action will begin to correct what is both a human rights abuse and distortive trade practice to improve the welfare of workers everywhere.
contentParagraphs.zh: 美国贸易代表杰米森·格里尔表示：“美国强制劳工进口禁令已有近一个世纪的历史，并且严格执行；我们的贸易伙伴早就该这样做了。” “今天的行动将开始纠正侵犯人权和扭曲贸易行为，以改善世界各地工人的福利。

contentParagraphs.en: The new tariffs will take effect just as temporary 10% worldwide tariffs expire at 12:01 a.m. Friday. Trump had turned to those temporary levies after the Supreme Court struck down his biggest and boldest tariffs in February.
contentParagraphs.zh: 新关税将于周五凌晨 12:01 生效，全球 10% 临时关税即将到期。最高法院在二月份推翻了特朗普最大规模、最大胆的关税后，特朗普转向了这些临时关税。

contentParagraphs.en: Now he's tapping more durable tariffs under Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974, which permits the president to impose import taxes and other sanctions against countries found to engage in "unjustifiable," "unreasonable" or "discriminatory" trade practices. Trump used Section 301 to impose big tariffs on China in his first term, and they survived court challenges.
contentParagraphs.zh: 现在，他正在根据 1974 年贸易法第 301 条征收更持久的关税，该条款允许总统对被发现从事“不合理”、“不合理”或“歧视性”贸易行为的国家征收进口税和其他制裁。特朗普在第一个任期内利用301条款对中国征收高额关税，并在法庭挑战中幸存下来。

contentParagraphs.en: More Section 301 tariffs are likely coming: The U.S. Trade Representative's office has launched a probe into whether 16 countries — accounting for 70% of U.S. imports — have overproduced goods, pushing down prices and putting U.S. companies at a disadvantage in global markets. The administration has yet to complete that investigation.
contentParagraphs.zh: 更多301条款关税可能即将出台：美国贸易代表办公室已启动调查，调查占美国进口70%的16个国家是否存在商品生产过剩、压低价格并使美国公司在全球市场处于不利地位的情况。政府尚未完成这项调查。

contentParagraphs.en: Trump, who argues that high tariffs will revive American manufacturing, last year overturned decades of U.S. policy that favored lower tariffs and ever-freer trade. Invoking the 1977 International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA), he imposed double-digit tariffs on imports from almost every country on Earth, saying America's longstanding trade deficit amounted to a national emergency.
contentParagraphs.zh: 特朗普认为高关税将重振美国制造业，去年他推翻了美国数十年来有利于降低关税和更加自由贸易的政策。他援引 1977 年《国际紧急经济权力法案》(IEEPA)，对地球上几乎每个国家的进口产品征收两位数的关税，称美国长期的贸易逆差已构成国家紧急状态。

contentParagraphs.en: But the Supreme Court ruled that IEEPA did not authorize tariffs. The decision forced the administration to pay refunds to importers that had paid the tariffs.
contentParagraphs.zh: 但最高法院裁定 IEEPA 未授权征收关税。该决定迫使政府向已支付关税的进口商退款。

contentParagraphs.en: In response, Trump announced 10% worldwide tariffs under Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974. But he can only use Section 122 levies for 150 days; time runs out on them Friday.
contentParagraphs.zh: 作为回应，特朗普宣布根据 1974 年贸易法第 122 条在全球范围内征收 10% 的关税。但他只能使用 122 条征税期限 150 天；周五他们的时间就到了。

contentParagraphs.en: The administration initially proposed the forced labor tariffs last month. Since then, some countries have tightened forced labor enforcement and qualified for lower tariffs, said a senior administration official who spoke under condition of anonymity. For example, the official said, the tariff on imports from India initially was set at 12.5% but now will be 10%.
contentParagraphs.zh: 政府最初于上个月提出了强迫劳动关税。一位不愿透露姓名的高级政府官员表示，自那以后，一些国家加强了强迫劳动执法，并有资格获得较低关税。例如，该官员表示，原定对印度进口产品的关税为12.5%，但现在为10%。

contentParagraphs.en: Some products — including oil and gas and fertilizer — are exempted from the new tariffs announced Thursday. Also being spared are products that qualify for duty-free status under the US-Mexico-Canada Agreement, the North American trade pact Trump negotiated in his first term.
contentParagraphs.zh: 一些产品——包括石油、天然气和化肥——不受周四宣布的新关税的影响。同样不受限制的还有根据《美国-墨西哥-加拿大协定》（特朗普在其第一个任期内谈判达成的北美贸易协定）有资格获得免税地位的产品。

contentParagraphs.en: Tariffs are paid by companies in the United States that import foreign products. The importers usually try to pass along the cost by charging consumers higher prices. Americans are already frustrated by the high cost of living. So the administration is taking a risk in rolling out new tariffs ahead of the Nov. 3 midterm elections.
contentParagraphs.zh: 关税由进口外国产品的美国公司支付。进口商通常试图通过向消费者收取更高的价格来转嫁成本。美国人已经对高昂的生活成本感到沮丧。因此，政府在 11 月 3 日中期选举之前推出新关税是冒险的。

contentParagraphs.en: Human rights watchers say that it's reasonable to be skeptical of the motivation behind the tariffs. But they say the levies could make an impact on the problem of forced labor.
contentParagraphs.zh: 人权观察人士表示，对关税背后的动机持怀疑态度是合理的。但他们表示，征税可能会对强迫劳动问题产生影响。

contentParagraphs.en: Forced labor is defined by the International Labor Organization Forced Labor Convention of 1930 as "all work or service which is exacted from any person under the menace of any penalty and for which the said person has not offered himself (or herself) voluntarily."
contentParagraphs.zh: 国际劳工组织 1930 年《强迫劳动公约》将强迫劳动定义为“以任何惩罚为威胁而强迫任何人从事且该人并非自愿从事的所有工作或服务”。

contentParagraphs.en: According to the latest statistics from the ILO, which is a U.N. agency focused on human and labor rights, about 27.6 million people were in forced labor worldwide on any given day in 2021.
contentParagraphs.zh: 根据国际劳工组织（专注于人权和劳工权利的联合国机构）的最新统计数据，2021 年全球每天约有 2760 万人处于强迫劳动状态。

contentParagraphs.en: "We've gone on record for years now advocating for import bans, not as a magic bullet, it's not a silver bullet, but as a potentially effective tool in combating forced labor across the globe," said Martina Vandenberg, founder and president of The Human Trafficking Legal Center.
contentParagraphs.zh: 人口贩运法律中心创始人兼总裁玛蒂娜·范登伯格 (Martina Vandenberg) 表示：“多年来，我们一直公开倡导进口禁令，这不是灵丹妙药，也不是灵丹妙药，而是打击全球强迫劳动的潜在有效工具。”

contentParagraphs.en: "It's possible to be extremely critical of tariffs, as we are, and to be very concerned about blanket tariffs used as bludgeons against countries," she said. "And yet I think it's undeniable that there is a significant response in terms of the adoption of import bans."
contentParagraphs.zh: “我们可能会像我们一样对关税持极端批评态度，并对被用作针对各国的大棒的一揽子关税感到非常担忧，”她说。 “但我认为不可否认的是，在采取进口禁令方面出现了重大反应。”

contentParagraphs.en: However Vandenberg and her organization urged in testimony that the tariffs be implemented in a phased approach to give countries time to implement a ban or a plan for enforcement.
contentParagraphs.zh: 然而，范登堡和她的组织在证词中敦促，关税应分阶段实施，以便各国有时间实施禁令或执行计划。

contentParagraphs.en: "Our concern is that the import bans will be thin slips of paper with no enforcement," she said. "Countries need time to build import ban mechanisms that are meaningful and enforceable."
contentParagraphs.zh: “我们担心的是，进口禁令将​​只是一纸空文，得不到执行，”她说。 “各国需要时间建立有意义且可执行的进口禁令机制。”

contentParagraphs.en: Kenya Davis, partner at Boies Schiller Flexner, said the Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act, a U.S. federal law passed in 2021 that prohibits the importation of goods made wholly or in part in China's Xinjiang region or by designated entities, is the most significant legislation related to forced labor that the U.S. has passed before the tariffs.
contentParagraphs.zh: Boies Schiller Flexner律师事务所合伙人肯尼娅·戴维斯(Kenya Davis)表示，《维吾尔强迫劳动预防法》是美国于2021年通过的一项联邦法律，禁止进口全部或部分在中国新疆地区或指定实体制造的商品，是美国在征收关税之前通过的与强迫劳动相关的最重要的立法。

contentParagraphs.en: "The level of effectiveness is certainly up for debate, but it certainly has drawn attention to the issue of labor trafficking and forced labor," she said. "And so, if nothing else, these import bans will serve that function of bringing greater awareness to forced labor."
contentParagraphs.zh: 她说：“有效性水平肯定有争议，但它确实引起了人们对劳动力贩运和强迫劳动问题的关注。” “因此，如果不出意外的话，这些进口禁令将​​起到提高人们对强迫劳动的认识的作用。”

contentParagraphs.en: But without a "comprehensive approach," that provides transparency about what the investigations consisted of, and programs that provide countries aid in enforcing
contentParagraphs.zh: 但如果没有“全面的方法”，就无法提供调查内容的透明度，以及为各国提供执行援助的计划

articles.title: Invasive species cause more harm in the Global South
articles.summary: Invasive species can wreak havoc on ecosystems. A new study in Science suggests low- and middle-income countries in the Global South experience greater harms.
articles.titleZh: 入侵物种对南半球造成更大危害
articles.summaryZh: 入侵物种会对生态系统造成严重破坏。 《科学》杂志的一项新研究表明，南半球的低收入和中等收入国家遭受的伤害更大。

contentParagraphs.en: Invasive species cause more harm in the Global South
contentParagraphs.zh: 入侵物种对南半球造成更大危害

contentParagraphs.en: <iframe src="https://www.npr.org/player/embed/nx-s1-5902194/nx-s1-9861061" width="100%" height="290" frameborder="0" scrolling="no" title="NPR embedded audio player">

contentParagraphs.en: Invasive species can wreak havoc on ecosystems. A new study in Science suggests low- and middle-income countries in the Global South experience greater harms.
contentParagraphs.zh: 入侵物种会对生态系统造成严重破坏。 《科学》杂志的一项新研究表明，南半球的低收入和中等收入国家遭受的伤害更大。

contentParagraphs.en: AILSA CHANG, HOST: Plants and animals often hitch rides on airplanes and cargo ships. These newcomers can devastate crops and wipe out native species. And historically, these invasive species were seen as a bigger problem for wealthier countries. But new research in the journal Science suggests that poorer countries experience more harm. NPR's Jonathan Lambert has more.JONATHAN LAMBERT, BYLINE: Invasive species can be devastatingly opportunistic. Plopped into a new environment, they're often free of the species that keep them in check back home and can go hog wild. Sven Bacher is an ecologist at the University of Fribourg. He points to the example of the fast-growing plant, the water hyacinth.SVEN BACHER: Which covers entire lake ecosystems, smothering the entire lake, filtering out all the light that gets to the ground. The fish are dying. Local fisheries actually have to give up.LAMBERT: It's one example of thousands that scientists have studied in recent decades. The bulk of that research has happened in wealthier countries in North America and Europe, sometimes referred to as the Global North. That focus...BACHER: Might have created the impression that this is actually a problem rather of developed countries with all the economy and all the exchange of goods and so on.LAMBERT: But these biological invasions happen in the Global South too. Water hyacinth, for example, is choking off waterways in Nigeria right now. And while there are way fewer studies outside of wealthy countries, there are some. Bacher and his team cooked up a way of comparing the impact in different regions by averaging across studies.BACHER: You can get a value that you can directly compare.LAMBERT: He and his colleagues analyzed data from over 22,000 instances of invasive species causing some kind of change in a natural environment. They looked to see on average whether these impacts were small or large in a given region for a given species. The pattern was striking.BACHER: You see that regions in the Global South - and not just some, like all over the Global South - suddenly, it shows that the impacts on average are much higher.LAMBERT: That pattern wasn't entirely surprising to Anibal Pauchard. He's an ecologist at the University of Concepcion in Chile. He praised the study and thinks it offers a couple early hints as to why invasive species could be causing more problems in low- and middle-income countries.ANIBAL PAUCHARD: Countries or communities with lower resources will be more impacted because their lifetimes are dependent on natural resources, and also because they don't have the tools to avoid or adapt to the changes caused by invasive species.LAMBERT: In fact, the study found that the weaker a government's capacity to respond, the worse the impact of an invasive species. Also Pauchard thinks that many of these countries have more biodiversity to lose.PAUCHARD: Biodiversity is under threat, and changes are very visible. Many of these countries have been changing only in the last hundred years or 200 years. Instead, Europe has changed for a long time.LAMBERT: So a single invasive species can cause a lot of harm. He points to eucalyptus trees, which have invaded many parts of South America and Africa. These trees can take over and suck up a lot of water.PAUCHARD: In the Global North, you can find, you know, new ways to get water from more faraway resources. But in the Global South, you're really dependent on the water that gets really close to your community.LAMBERT: Invasive species are a global problem, but Pauchard says this study shows how solutions must be tailored to specific places. Figuring out those solutions, he says, will require greater cooperation between the Global North and the Global South.Jonathan Lambert, NPR News.
contentParagraphs.zh: 主持人 AILSA CHANG：植物和动物经常搭飞机和货船的便车。这些新来者可能会破坏农作物并消灭本地物种。从历史上看，这些入侵物种被视为富裕国家面临的更大问题。但《科学》杂志上的新研究表明，较贫穷的国家遭受的伤害更大。 NPR 的乔纳森·兰伯特 (Jonathan Lambert) 报道了更多。乔纳森·兰伯特连线：入侵物种可能会带来毁灭性的机会主义。当它们进入一个新的环境时，它们通常会摆脱那些在家里限制它们的物种，并可能变得狂野起来。斯文·巴赫 (Sven Bacher) 是弗里堡大学的生态学家。他以快速生长的植物水葫芦为例。 SVEN BACHER：它覆盖了整个湖泊生态系统，窒息了整个湖泊，过滤掉了到达地面的所有光线。鱼快死了。当地渔业实际上不得不放弃。 兰伯特：这是科学家近几十年来研究的数千个例子之一。大部分研究发生在北美和欧洲较富裕的国家，有时被称为“全球北方”。这个焦点...巴彻：可能给人留下这样的印象：这实际上是一个问题，而不是发达国家的所有经济和所有商品交换等等。兰伯特：但这些生物入侵也发生在全球南方。例如，水葫芦现在正在堵塞尼日利亚的水道。尽管富裕国家以外的研究要少得多，但还是有一些的。 Bacher 和他的团队想出了一种通过对研究进行平均来比较不同地区影响的方法。 BACHER：你可以获得一个可以直接比较的值。 LAMBERT：他和他的同事分析了 22,000 多个入侵物种实例的数据，这些物种导致自然环境发生某种变化。他们平均观察特定区域内特定物种的这些影响是小还是大。这种模式是惊人的。 BACHER：你会看到南半球的一些地区——而不仅仅是一些地区，比如整个南半球——突然间，它表明平均影响要高得多。 LAMBERT：这种模式对 Anibal Pauchard 来说并不完全令人惊讶。他是智利康塞普西翁大学的生态学家。他赞扬这项研究，并认为它提供了一些早期提示，说明为什么入侵物种可能在低收入和中等收入国家造成更多问题。 阿尼巴尔·帕查德：资源较少的国家或社区将受到更大的影响，因为它们的一生依赖于自然资源，也因为他们没有工具来避免或适应入侵物种造成的变化。兰伯特：事实上，研究发现，政府的应对能力越弱，入侵物种的影响就越严重。入侵物种。波查德还认为，其中许多国家将失去更多的生物多样性。波查德：生物多样性正受到威胁，而且变化非常明显。其中许多国家仅在过去一百年或 200 年才发生变化。相反，欧洲已经发生了很长一段时间的变化。 兰伯特：所以单一的入侵物种可能会造成很多伤害。他指出桉树已入侵南美洲和非洲的许多地区。这些树木可以接管并吸收大量的水。 PAUCARD：在北半球，你可以找到从更遥远的资源中获取水的新方法。但在南半球，你确实依赖于离你的社区非常近的水。 兰伯特：入侵物种是一个全球性问题，但帕查德说这项研究表明解决方案必须针对特定地区量身定制。他说，找出这些解决方案将需要北半球和南半球之间加强合作。乔纳森·兰伯特，NPR 新闻。
